<skill_content name="pinchtab">
# Skill: pinchtab

# Pinchtab

Fast, lightweight browser control for AI agents via HTTP + accessibility tree.

**Security Note:** Pinchtab runs entirely locally. It does not contact external services, send telemetry, or exfiltrate data. However, it controls a real Chrome instance — if pointed at a profile with saved logins, agents can access authenticated sites. Always use a dedicated empty profile and set BRIDGE_TOKEN when exposing the API. See [TRUST.md](TRUST.md) for the full security model.

## How Pinchtab Works (Two-Tier Architecture)

Pinchtab runs as **two separate layers**. You must understand this before making any API calls:

| Layer         | Port                      | What it is                                                        |
| ------------- | ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Dashboard** | `9867`                    | Manages profiles and instances. No Chrome here.                   |
| **Instance**  | `9868` (or auto-assigned) | The actual Chrome browser process. All browser API calls go here. |

Running `pinchtab` by itself **only starts the dashboard**. Chrome does not launch. Every browser API call (`/navigate`, `/snapshot`, `/tabs`, etc.) will return `503 get targets: context canceled` until you start a profile instance.

**Never expose to 0.0.0.0 without a token. Never point at your daily Chrome profile.**

For advanced env var configuration, see [references/env.md](references/env.md).

## Scripts

Two PowerShell scripts in `.opencode/skills/pinchtab/scripts/` cover the full task lifecycle:

| Script      | Purpose                                                                   |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `setup.ps1` | Start dashboard + instance, recover from crashes, set `$env:PINCHTAB_URL` |
| `stop.ps1`  | Close the Chrome window and stop the instance                             |

## Startup Flow (Do This First)

Run `setup.ps1` before any browser task. It starts the dashboard if needed, creates the `automation` profile if missing, starts the instance, detects and recovers from crashes, and waits for Chrome to be healthy.

```powershell
# Default (headed -- visible Chrome window)
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ".opencode/skills/pinchtab/scripts/setup.ps1"

# Headless (no window)
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ".opencode/skills/pinchtab/scripts/setup.ps1" -Mode headless
```

The script sets `$env:PINCHTAB_URL` in the current session and prints the instance URL. After it completes, the pinchtab CLI and API are both ready — no further configuration needed.

To stop Chrome when done, run `stop.ps1`:

```powershell
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ".opencode/skills/pinchtab/scripts/stop.ps1"
```

For manual profile management, see [references/profiles.md](references/profiles.md).

## What a Snapshot Looks Like

After calling `/snapshot?format=json&filter=interactive`, you get the page's accessibility tree — a flat list of nodes with refs:

```json
{
  "count": 3,
  "nodes": [
    { "ref": "e0", "role": "link", "name": "Sign In", "depth": 3, "nodeId": 14 },
    { "ref": "e1", "role": "textbox", "name": "Email", "depth": 4, "nodeId": 17 },
    { "ref": "e2", "role": "button", "name": "Submit", "depth": 4, "nodeId": 21 }
  ],
  "title": "Login Page",
  "url": "https://example.com/login"
}
```

With `?format=compact` the response is plain text (not JSON), one node per line:

```
# Login Page | https://example.com/login | 3 nodes
e0:link "Sign In"
e1:textbox "Email"
e2:button "Submit"
```

Then you act on refs: `click e0`, `type e1 "user@example.com"`, `press e2 Enter`.

## Core Workflow

**Use the CLI for everything by default. Only use the HTTP API when the CLI cannot do what you need.**

The CLI is simpler, blocks until the page is fully loaded, and uses fewer tokens. The HTTP API is a fallback for tab-specific actions (`click`, `type`, `press` on a non-default tab) and advanced features like upload, cookies, and stealth mode. See [references/api.md](references/api.md) for those cases.

The typical agent loop:

1. **Navigate** to a URL
2. **Snapshot** the accessibility tree (get refs)
3. **Act** on refs (click, type, press)
4. **Snapshot** again to see results

Refs (e.g. `e0`, `e5`, `e12`) are cached per tab after each snapshot — no need to re-snapshot before every action unless the page changed significantly.

### Quick examples (CLI)

After running the setup script, `$env:PINCHTAB_URL` points to the instance. Use the CLI:

```powershell
pinchtab nav https://example.com
pinchtab snap -i -c              # interactive + compact (most token-efficient)
pinchtab click e5
pinchtab type e12 hello world
pinchtab press Enter
pinchtab text                    # readable text (~1K tokens)
pinchtab ss -o page.jpg          # screenshot
pinchtab eval "document.title"   # run JavaScript
pinchtab pdf --tab TAB_ID -o page.pdf
```

> **Note:** `pinchtab snap -i -c` prints a warning at the end:
> `warning: error unmarshaling response: invalid character '#' looking for beginning of value`
> This is a known CLI bug — compact format returns plain text, not JSON, and the CLI tries to parse it anyway. **The snapshot output above the warning is correct. Ignore the warning.**

## Token Cost Guide

| Method                         | Typical tokens | When to use                                                                      |
| ------------------------------ | -------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `/text`                        | ~800           | Reading page content                                                             |
| `/snapshot?filter=interactive` | ~3,600         | Finding buttons/links to click                                                   |
| `/snapshot?diff=true`          | varies         | Multi-step workflows (only changes)                                              |
| `/snapshot?format=compact`     | ~56-64% less   | One-line-per-node, best efficiency                                               |
| `/snapshot`                    | ~10,500        | Full page understanding                                                          |
| `/screenshot`                  | ~2K (vision)   | Visual verification                                                              |
| `/tabs/TAB_ID/pdf`             | 0 (binary)     | Export page as PDF — TAB_ID is the CDP hex id from `/tabs`, not the `tab_xxx` id |

**Strategy**: Start with `?filter=interactive&format=compact`. Use `?diff=true` on subsequent snapshots. Use `/text` when you only need readable content. Full `/snapshot` only when needed.

## Agent Optimization

**Validated Feb 2026**: Testing with AI agents revealed a critical pattern for reliable, token-efficient scraping.

### The navigate-then-snapshot pattern

Always capture the `tabId` from the navigate response and pass it explicitly to every subsequent snapshot/action. Without it, snapshots return stale content from the previously active tab.

**Two tab ID formats exist — only one works for snapshots:**

| Source                                | Format                                       | Works with `/snapshot`?      |
| ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| `pinchtab nav` / `/navigate` response | `tab_3ff7204d`                               | Yes                          |
| `pinchtab tabs` / `/tabs` response    | `3683349CC79C24D2C25DC1D7DF467E2A` (CDP hex) | No — returns "tab not found" |

Always use the `tab_xxx` ID from the navigate response. Never pass the hex ID from `/tabs`.

```powershell
# Navigate -- tabId is in the JSON output
pinchtab nav https://example.com
# {
#   "tabId": "tab_b988cdbf",   <-- capture this
#   "title": "Example Domain",
#   "url": "https://example.com/"
# }

# --tab is supported by snap and pdf only
pinchtab snap -i -c --tab tab_b988cdbf
```

**`--tab` is only supported by `snap` and `pdf`.** It does NOT work with `click`, `type`, `press`, or other action commands — the flag is silently ignored and the CLI acts on the default tab.

For actions on a specific tab, use the HTTP API directly with the tab ID in the URL path:

```powershell
# Navigate via CLI -- captures tabId from JSON output
$nav = pinchtab nav https://example.com | ConvertFrom-Json
$tabId = $nav.tabId   # e.g. "tab_b988cdbf"

# Snapshot -- CLI --tab works here
pinchtab snap -i -c --tab $tabId

# Actions on a specific tab -- CLI does not support --tab here, use HTTP API
# (In PowerShell, curl is an alias for Invoke-WebRequest -- use curl.exe explicitly)
& curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs/$tabId/click?ref=e0"
& curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs/$tabId/type?ref=e1" `
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"text":"hello world"}'
& curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs/$tabId/press?key=Enter"
```

**Token savings:** 93% reduction when using prescriptive instructions vs. exploratory agent approach.

## Tips

- **`--tab` flag: only works with `snap` and `pdf`** — `click`, `type`, `press`, and other action commands ignore it silently. For actions on a specific tab, use the HTTP API: `& curl.exe -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs/$tabId/click?ref=e0"`
- **Tab IDs: always use `tab_xxx` from navigate, never the hex ID from `/tabs`** — the CDP hex IDs returned by `/tabs` do not work with `/snapshot` or any action endpoint and will return "tab not found"
- Refs are stable between snapshot and actions — no need to re-snapshot before clicking
- After navigation or major page changes, take a new snapshot for fresh refs
- Chrome profile is persistent — cookies/logins carry over between runs
- Pass `"blockImages": true` in the `/navigate` JSON body for read-heavy tasks
- **Wait 3+ seconds after navigate before snapshot** — only needed when using raw `curl` for `/navigate`, which returns as soon as the URL is accepted, not when the page is fully loaded. The `pinchtab nav` CLI waits for full page load before returning — no sleep needed when using the CLI.

<type>file</type>
<content>1: # Pinchtab API Reference
2: 
3: Base URL for all examples: `$env:PINCHTAB_URL` (e.g. `http://localhost:9868` — the **instance** port, set by `setup.ps1`)
4: 
5: > **Note:** Port `9867` is the dashboard (profile management only). All browser API calls go to the instance on port `9868`. In PowerShell, `curl` is an alias for `Invoke-WebRequest` — always use `& curl.exe` or `Invoke-RestMethod` explicitly.
6: 
7: > **CLI first:** Use the `pinchtab` CLI for all standard tasks (nav, snap, click, type, press, text, screenshot, eval). Only use this HTTP API reference when the CLI cannot do what you need — tab-specific actions, upload, stealth mode, batch actions, cookies, or advanced PDF options.
8: 
9: ## Navigate
10: 
11: ```powershell
12: # CLI: pinchtab nav https://example.com
13: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/navigate" `
14:   -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
15:   -d '{"url": "https://example.com"}'
16: 
17: # With options: custom timeout, block images, open in new tab
18: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/navigate" `
19:   -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
20:   -d '{"url": "https://example.com", "timeout": 60, "blockImages": true, "newTab": true}'
21: 
22: # PowerShell (Invoke-RestMethod parses JSON automatically)
23: $nav = Invoke-RestMethod -Uri "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/navigate" `
24:   -Method Post -ContentType "application/json" `
25:   -Body '{"url": "https://example.com"}'
26: $tabId = $nav.tabId   # use this for all subsequent calls
27: ```
28: 
29: ## Snapshot (accessibility tree)
30: 
31: ```powershell
32: # CLI: pinchtab snap [-i] [-c] [-d] [-s main] [--max-tokens 2000]
33: 
34: # Full tree
35: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/snapshot"
36: 
37: # Interactive elements only (buttons, links, inputs) -- much smaller
38: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/snapshot?filter=interactive"
39: 
40: # Compact format -- one-line-per-node, 56-64% fewer tokens (recommended)
41: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/snapshot?format=compact"
42: 
43: # Smart diff -- only changes since last snapshot (massive token savings)
44: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/snapshot?diff=true"
45: 
46: # Scope to CSS selector (e.g. main content only)
47: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/snapshot?selector=main"
48: 
49: # Truncate to ~N tokens
50: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/snapshot?maxTokens=2000"
51: 
52: # Combine for maximum efficiency
53: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/snapshot?format=compact&filter=interactive&selector=main&maxTokens=2000"
54: 
55: # Target a specific tab (use tab_xxx id from /navigate response)
56: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/snapshot?format=compact&filter=interactive&tabId=tab_b988cdbf"
57: ```
58: 
59: Returns flat JSON array of nodes with `ref`, `role`, `name`, `depth`, `value`, `nodeId`.
60: 
61: **Token optimization**: Use `?format=compact` for best token efficiency. Add `?filter=interactive` for action-oriented tasks (~75% fewer nodes). Use `?selector=main` to scope to relevant content. Use `?maxTokens=2000` to cap output. Use `?diff=true` on multi-step workflows to see only changes. Combine all params freely.
62: 
63: ## Act on elements
64: 
65: ```powershell
66: # CLI: pinchtab click e5 / pinchtab type e12 hello / pinchtab press Enter
67: 
68: # Click by ref
69: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
70:   -d '{"kind": "click", "ref": "e5"}'
71: 
72: # Type into element
73: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
74:   -d '{"kind": "type", "ref": "e12", "text": "hello world"}'
75: 
76: # Press a key
77: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
78:   -d '{"kind": "press", "key": "Enter"}'
79: 
80: # Focus an element
81: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
82:   -d '{"kind": "focus", "ref": "e3"}'
83: 
84: # Fill (set value directly, no keystrokes)
85: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
86:   -d '{"kind": "fill", "selector": "#email", "text": "user@example.com"}'
87: 
88: # Hover (trigger dropdowns/tooltips)
89: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
90:   -d '{"kind": "hover", "ref": "e8"}'
91: 
92: # Select dropdown option
93: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
94:   -d '{"kind": "select", "ref": "e10", "value": "option2"}'
95: 
96: # Scroll to element
97: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
98:   -d '{"kind": "scroll", "ref": "e20"}'
99: 
100: # Scroll by pixels (infinite scroll pages)
101: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
102:   -d '{"kind": "scroll", "scrollY": 800}'
103: 
104: # Click and wait for navigation (link clicks)
105: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/action" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
106:   -d '{"kind": "click", "ref": "e5", "waitNav": true}'
107: ```
108: 
109: ## Batch actions
110: 
111: ```powershell
112: # Execute multiple actions in sequence
113: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/actions" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
114:   -d '{"actions":[{"kind":"click","ref":"e3"},{"kind":"type","ref":"e3","text":"hello"},{"kind":"press","key":"Enter"}]}'
115: 
116: # Stop on first error (default: false)
117: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/actions" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
118:   -d '{"tabId":"tab_b988cdbf","actions":[{"kind":"click","ref":"e3"}],"stopOnError":true}'
119: ```
120: 
121: ## Extract text
122: 
123: ```powershell
124: # CLI: pinchtab text [--raw]
125: 
126: # Readability mode (default) -- strips nav/footer/ads
127: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/text"
128: 
129: # Raw innerText
130: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/text?mode=raw"
131: ```
132: 
133: Returns `{url, title, text}`. Cheapest option (~1K tokens for most pages).
134: 
135: ## PDF export
136: 
137: > **Note:** PDF export uses the CDP hex tab ID (from `/tabs`), not the `tab_xxx` id.
138: 
139: ```powershell
140: # CLI: pinchtab pdf --tab TAB_ID [-o file.pdf] [--landscape] [--scale 0.8]
141: 
142: # Raw PDF bytes saved to file
143: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs/TAB_ID/pdf?raw=true" -o page.pdf
144: 
145: # Landscape with custom scale
146: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs/TAB_ID/pdf?landscape=true&scale=0.8&raw=true" -o page.pdf
147: 
148: # Custom paper size
149: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs/TAB_ID/pdf?paperWidth=8.5&paperHeight=11&raw=true" -o custom.pdf
150: 
151: # Export specific pages
152: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs/TAB_ID/pdf?pageRanges=1-5&raw=true" -o pages.pdf
153: ```
154: 
155: **Query Parameters:**
156: 
157: | Param                     | Type   | Default | Description                                    |
158: | ------------------------- | ------ | ------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
159: | `paperWidth`              | float  | 8.5     | Paper width in inches                          |
160: | `paperHeight`             | float  | 11.0    | Paper height in inches                         |
161: | `landscape`               | bool   | false   | Landscape orientation                          |
162: | `marginTop`               | float  | 0.4     | Top margin in inches                           |
163: | `marginBottom`            | float  | 0.4     | Bottom margin in inches                        |
164: | `marginLeft`              | float  | 0.4     | Left margin in inches                          |
165: | `marginRight`             | float  | 0.4     | Right margin in inches                         |
166: | `scale`                   | float  | 1.0     | Print scale (0.1–2.0)                          |
167: | `pageRanges`              | string | all     | Pages to export (e.g., `1-3,5`)                |
168: | `displayHeaderFooter`     | bool   | false   | Show header and footer                         |
169: | `headerTemplate`          | string | —       | HTML template for header                       |
170: | `footerTemplate`          | string | —       | HTML template for footer                       |
171: | `preferCSSPageSize`       | bool   | false   | Honor CSS `@page` size                         |
172: | `generateTaggedPDF`       | bool   | false   | Generate accessible/tagged PDF                 |
173: | `generateDocumentOutline` | bool   | false   | Embed document outline                         |
174: | `output`                  | string | JSON    | `file` to save to disk, default returns base64 |
175: | `path`                    | string | auto    | Custom file path (with `output=file`)          |
176: | `raw`                     | bool   | false   | Return raw PDF bytes instead of JSON           |
177: 
178: ## Download files
179: 
180: ```powershell
181: # Raw bytes saved to file (uses browser session/cookies/stealth)
182: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/download?url=https://site.com/report.pdf&raw=true" -o report.pdf
183: 
184: # Save directly to disk (server-side)
185: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/download?url=https://site.com/export.csv&output=file&path=C:/tmp/export.csv"
186: ```
187: 
188: ## Upload files
189: 
190: ```powershell
191: # Upload a local file to a file input
192: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/upload?tabId=tab_b988cdbf" `
193:   -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
194:   -d '{"selector": "input[type=file]", "paths": ["C:/tmp/photo.jpg"]}'
195: ```
196: 
197: Sets files on `<input type=file>` elements via CDP. Fires `change` events.
198: 
199: ## Screenshot
200: 
201: ```powershell
202: # CLI: pinchtab ss [-o file.jpg] [-q 80]
203: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/screenshot?raw=true" -o screenshot.jpg
204: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/screenshot?raw=true&quality=50" -o screenshot.jpg
205: ```
206: 
207: ## Evaluate JavaScript
208: 
209: ```powershell
210: # CLI: pinchtab eval "document.title"
211: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/evaluate" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
212:   -d '{"expression": "document.title"}'
213: ```
214: 
215: ## Tab management
216: 
217: ```powershell
218: # CLI: pinchtab tabs / pinchtab tabs close <id>
219: 
220: # List tabs
221: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tabs"
222: 
223: # Open new tab
224: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tab" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
225:   -d '{"action": "new", "url": "https://example.com"}'
226: 
227: # Close tab (use CDP hex id from /tabs)
228: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/tab" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
229:   -d '{"action": "close", "tabId": "CDP_HEX_ID"}'
230: ```
231: 
232: Multi-tab: pass `?tabId=tab_xxx` (from navigate) to snapshot/screenshot/text, or `"tabId"` in POST body.
233: 
234: ## Cookies
235: 
236: ```powershell
237: # Get cookies for current page
238: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/cookies"
239: 
240: # Set cookies
241: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/cookies" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
242:   -d '{"url":"https://example.com","cookies":[{"name":"session","value":"abc123"}]}'
243: ```
244: 
245: ## Stealth
246: 
247: ```powershell
248: # Check stealth status and score
249: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/stealth/status"
250: 
251: # Rotate browser fingerprint
252: & curl.exe -s -X POST "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/fingerprint/rotate" -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
253:   -d '{"os":"windows"}'
254: # os: "windows", "mac", or omit for random
255: ```
256: 
257: ## Health check
258: 
259: ```powershell
260: & curl.exe -s "$env:PINCHTAB_URL/health"
261: ```

(End of file - total 261 lines)

Write-Host "Comment URL: $($response.result)"
